subject: [PATCH v2 3/4] elf: align ET_DYN base to max folio size for PTE coalescing

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the alignment of PT_LOAD segments in ELF binaries, specifically for PIE (Position-Independent Executable) binaries. The reviewer had pointed out that the current implementation does not properly align the virtual address to the maximum folio size supported by the page cache, which can prevent hardware PTE coalescing and PMD mappings for large folios. The author has explained their reasoning behind the changes they made in the patch, including the use of the largest power-of-2 that fits within the segment size, capped by what the page cache will allocate. They have also clarified that the segment size check is used to avoid reducing ASLR entropy for small binaries. The author's response suggests that the patch will need further revision to address the reviewer's concerns.

reviews.author: Matthew Wilcox
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Matthew Wilcox asked about the circumstances under which bprm->file can be NULL and suggested renaming 'filp' to 'file' for consistency with other kernel code.

Reviewer Matthew Wilcox suggested refactoring the code to extract a separate function for alignment calculations and pointed out an improvement in a similar implementation elsewhere.
reviews.raw_body:
Under what circumstances can bprm->file be NULL?

Also we tend to prefer the name "file" rather than "filp" for new code
(yes, there's a lot of old code out there).

---

Can this not all be factored out into a different function?  Also, I
think it was done a bit better here:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/20260313005211.882831-1-r@hev.cc/

+	if (!IS_ALIGNED(cmd->p_vaddr | cmd->p_offset, PMD_SIZE))
+		return false;

reviews.author: WANG Rui
reviews.summary:
Reviewer WANG Rui noted that the patch does not handle the case where the file offset is not aligned to the maximum folio size, which can lead to incorrect alignment of the ET_DYN base address.

Reviewer WANG Rui noted that the current implementation of maximum_alignment() returns a fixed value based on the filesystem, which can be overly conservative and miss opportunities for optimization, specifically mentioning that ext4 returns 8M while btrfs falls back to PAGE_SIZE.

Reviewer WANG Rui suggested considering aligning eligible segments to PMD_SIZE, allowing THP to quickly collapse them into large mappings, and proposed a reasonably sized PMD of <= 32M for virtual address alignment.

Reviewer WANG Rui noted that the patch does not handle the case where the PT_LOAD segment is smaller than the maximum folio size, potentially leading to incorrect alignment and affecting hardware PTE coalescing.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Usama,

On Fri, Mar 20, 2026 at 10:04\u202fPM Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev> wrote:

---

depend on the filesystem. It returns 8M on ext4, while on btrfs it always
falls back to PAGE_SIZE (it seems CONFIG_BTRFS_EXPERIMENTAL=y may change this).
This looks overly conservative and ends up missing practical optimization
opportunities.

---

In my patch [1], by aligning eligible segments to PMD_SIZE, THP can quickly
collapse them into large mappings with minimal warmup. That doesn\u2019t happen
with the current behavior. I think allowing a reasonably sized PMD (say <= 32M)
is worth considering. All we really need here is to ensure virtual address
alignment. The rest can be left to THP under always, which can decide whether
to collapse or not based on memory pressure and other factors.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/20260313005211.882831-1-r@hev.cc

---

Thanks,
Rui

reviews.author: Matthew Wilcox
reviews.summary: The reviewer pointed out that the patch assumes btrfs supports large folios without considering its experimental status and suggested that changes should not be rushed.
reviews.raw_body:
btrfs only supports large folios with CONFIG_BTRFS_EXPERIMENTAL.
I mean, it's only been five years since it was added to XFS, can't rush
these things.

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that the maximum_alignment() function's second argument is not necessary for this specific case, as it is only used in other places and not checked here, agreeing to remove it.

Author acknowledged a need to adjust alignment in maximum_alignment() function to accommodate max folio size, agreeing to make changes.
reviews.raw_body:
Yeah its unnecessary here. Its used in other places and this is never
checked, so we can remove it.

---

ack, will try and address this accordingly.


Thanks for the reviews!!

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged a concern about over-aligning binaries on filesystems that cannot support large folio alignment, and decided not to align beyond the reported page size by those filesystems.
reviews.raw_body:
mapping_max_folio_size() reflects what the page cache will actually
allocate for a given filesystem, since readahead caps folio allocation
at mapping_max_folio_order() (in page_cache_ra_order()). If btrfs
reports PAGE_SIZE, readahead won't allocate large folios for it, so
there are no large folios to coalesce PTEs for, aligning the binary
beyond that would only reduce ASLR entropy for no benefit.

I don't think we should over-align binaries on filesystems that can't
take advantage of it.

reviews.author: WANG Rui
reviews.summary: Reviewer WANG Rui noted that the patch may overlook another path to create huge page mappings for read-only code segments, specifically when using khugepaged and filesystems without large folio support, and requested clarification on whether using mapping_max_folio_size() is favorable in this case.
reviews.raw_body:
Ah, it looks like this might be overlooking another path that can create
huge page mappings for read-only code segments: even when the filesystem
(e.g. btrfs without experimental) didn't support large folios,
READ_ONLY_THP_FOR_FS still allowed read-only file-backed code segments
to be collapsed into huge page mappings via khugepaged.

As Wilcox pointed out, it may take quite some time for many filesystems
to gain full large folio support? So what I'm trying to clarify is that
using mapping_max_folio_size() on this path is not favorable for
khugepaged-based optimizations.

Thanks,
Rui

reviews.author: Matthew Wilcox
reviews.summary: Reviewer Matthew Wilcox pointed out that btrfs is not supporting large folios, which he believes should be changed, and also criticized the READ_ONLY_THP_FOR_FS hack as unnecessary and outdated.
reviews.raw_body:
Nono, that's not what I'm pointing out!  btrfs is simply not putting
in the effort to support large folios, and that needs to change.
READ_ONLY_THP_FOR_FS unnecessaily burdens the rest of the kernel.
It was a great hack for its time and paved the path for a lot of what
we have today, but it's time to remove it.

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged a potential naming issue in the patch and suggested renaming one of its functions.

Author expressed concern that the current alignment (32M) is too large and may reduce ASLR entropy, suggesting an alternative approach to use the maximum of SZ_2M and PMD_ORDER instead.
sentiment_signals: concern about potential impact on ASLR
reviews.raw_body:
ah yes, Thank you for pointing this out!

Maybe we should rename mapping_max_folio_size() to mapping_fault_max_folio_size().

---

ack

I am worried that 32M is too large and we lose out on a lot of ASLR bits.
Instead of PMD_ORDER, should we do max(SZ_2M, PMD_ORDER)?

2026-03-20.patch_summary: This patch aligns the base of PIE binaries (ET_DYN) to the maximum folio size supported by the page cache, enabling hardware PTE coalescing and PMD mappings for large folios. The alignment is determined by considering the maximum folio size and segment size, ensuring that file-offset-aligned folios map to properly aligned virtual addresses. This fix addresses an issue where readahead patches cannot benefit from contpte mapping due to improper virtual address alignment.
